items.q: Is a trade deficit necessarily bad?
items.correct: Not necessarily — it can reflect strong domestic demand, attractive investment opportunities, or borrowing to fund growth. Context matters more than the headline figure.
items.misconception: A trade deficit always means a country is losing wealth and failing economically.
items.random_wrong: A trade deficit means a country has no factories.
items.why: The US has run deficits for decades while growing. Trade deficits are balanced by capital inflows. Students treat deficits as automatic failure, missing the accounting identity and what it implies.

items.q: How does the sign of cross elasticity of demand (XED) reveal product relationships?
items.correct: Positive XED = substitutes (price of one rises, demand for the other rises); negative XED = complements (price of one rises, demand for the other falls); zero XED = unrelated goods.
items.misconception: The sign of XED doesn't matter — only the size does.
items.random_wrong: XED measures how often consumers cross-shop between stores.
items.why: The sign carries the qualitative information about the relationship — substitute vs complement. Size tells you the strength of the relationship. Students focus on magnitude and miss the categorical meaning of the sign.

items.correct: An initial injection of spending circulates as recipients spend portions, generating more income — total effect on GDP is larger than the initial injection.
items.misconception: The multiplier means government spending is always good because it multiplies wealth.
items.random_wrong: The multiplier is the rate at which interest compounds.
items.why: The multiplier is a mechanism, not a verdict. Whether to use it depends on conditions (slack in economy, marginal propensity to consume). Students confuse the descriptive concept with a policy prescription.

items.q: Bulk discounts for buying 100+ units are an example of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Second degree. “First degree” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Pigou identified how many degrees of price discrimination?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
